Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - DMSANET: DUAL MULTI SCALE ATTENTION NET WORK

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Attention mechanism of late has been quite popular in the computer vision community. A lot of work has been done to improve the performance of the network, although almost always it results in increased computational complexity. In this paper, we propose a new attention module that not only achieves the best performance but also has lesser parameters compared to most existing models. Our attention module can easily be integrated with other convolutional neural networks because of its lightweight nature. The proposed network named Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet) is comprised of two parts: the first part is used to extract features at various scales and aggregate them, the second part uses spatial and channel attention modules in parallel to adaptively integrate local features with their global dependencies. We benchmark our network performance for Image Classification on ImageNet dataset, Object Detection and Instance Segmentation both on MS COCO dataset.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The local receptive field of the human eye has led to the construction of convolutional neural networks which has powered much of the recent advances in computer vision. Multi scale architecture used in the famous InceptionNet (Szegedy et al., 2016) aggregates multi-scale information from different size convolutional kernels. Attention Networks has attracted a lot of attention recently as it allows the network to focus on only then essential aspects while ignoring the ones which are not useful (Li et al., 2019), (Cao et al., 2019) and (Li et al., 2019).

A lot of problems have been successfully tackled using attention mechanism in computer vision like image classification, image segmentation, object detection and image generation. Most of the attention mechanisms can be broadly classified into two types channel attention and spatial attention, both of which strengthens the original features by aggregating the same feature from all the positions with different aggregation strategies, transformations, and strengthening functions (Zhang et al., 2021).

Some of the work combined both these mechanism together and achieved better results (Cao et al., 2019) and (Woo et al., 2018). The computational burden was reduced by (Wang et al., 2020) using efficient channel attention and 1 _×_ 1 convolution. The most popular attention mechanism is the Squeeze-and Excitation module (Hu et al., 2018b), which can significantly improve the performance with a considerably low cost. The “channel shuffle” operator is used (Zhang and Yang, 2021) to enable information communication between the two branches. It uses a grouping strategy, which divides the input feature map into groups along the channel dimension.

## 2 RELATED WORK

There are two main problems which hinders the progress in this field: 1) Both spatial and channel attention as well as network using combination of two uses only local information while ignoring long range channel dependency, 2) The previous architectures fail to capture spatial information at different scales to be more robust and handle more complex problems. These two challenges were tackled by (Duta et al., 2020) and (Li et al., 2019) respectivly. The problem with these architectures is that the number of parameters increased considerably.

1

Pyramid Split Attention (PSA) (Zhang et al., 2021) has the ability to process the input tensor at multiple scales. A multi-scale pyramid convolution structure is used to integrate information at different scales on each channel-wise feature map. The channel-wise attention weight of the multi-scale feature maps are extracted hence long range channel dependency is done.

Non-Local block (Wang et al., 2018) is proposed to build a dense spatial feature map and capture the long-range dependency using non-local operations. (Li et al., 2019) used a dynamic selection attention mechanism that allows each neuron to adaptively adjust its receptive field size based on multiple scales of input feature map. (Fu et al., 2019) proposed a network to integrate local features with their global dependencies by summing these two attention modules from different branches.

Multi scale architectures have been used sucessfully for a lot of vision problems ( **?** ), (Hu et al., 2018b) and (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020). (Fu et al., 2019) adaptively integrated local features with their global dependencies by summing the two attention modules from different branches. (Hu et al., 2018a) used spatial extension using a depth-wise convolution to aggregate individual features. Our network borrows ideas from (Gao et al., 2018) which used a network to capture local cross-channel interactions.
The performance (in terms of accuracy) vs computational complexity (in terms of number of parameters) of the state of art attention modules is shown in Figure 1:

Figure 1: Comparing the accuracy of different attention methods with ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 as backbone in terms of accuracy and network parameters. The circles reflects the network parameters and FLOPs of different models. Our proposed network achieves higher accuracy while having less model complexity.

Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:

• A new attention module is proposed which aggregates feature information at various scales. Our network is scalable and can be easily plugged into various computer vision problems.

- Our network captures more contextual information using both spatial and channel attention at

- various scales.
• Our experiments demonstrate that our network outperforms previous state of the art with lesser computational cost.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 FEATURE GROUPING

Shuffle Attention module divides the input feature map into groups and uses Shuffle Unit to integrate the channel attention and spatial attention into one block for each group. The sub-features are aggregated and a “channel shuffle” operator is used for communicating the information between different sub-features.

For a given feature map _X ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ , where _C, H, W_ indicate the channel number, spatial height, and width, respectively, shuffle attention module divides _X_ into _G_ groups along the channel dimension, i.e., _X_ = [ _X_ 1 _, XG_ ] _, Xk ∈ R[C/G][×][H][×][W]_ . An attention module is used to weight the importance of each feature. The input of _Xk_ is split into two networks along the channel dimension _Xk_ 1 _, Xk_ 2 _∈ R[C/]_[2] _[G][×][H][×][W]_ . The first branch is used to produce a channel attention map by using the relationship of channels, while the second branch is used to generate a spatial attention map by using the spatial relationship of different features.

## 3.2 CHANNEL ATTENTION MODULE

## 3.5 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

We propose DMSA module with the goal to build more efficient and scalable architecture. The first part of our network borrows ideas from (Li et al., 2019) and (Zhang and Yang, 2021). An input feature map _X_ is splitted into _N_ parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has _C_ 0= _CS_ number of common channels, and the _i[th]_ feature map is _Xi ∈ R[C]_[0] _[×][H][×][W]_ . The individual features are fused before being passed to two different branches.

These two branches are comprised of position attention module and channel attention module as proposed in (Fu et al., 2019) for semantic segmentation. The second part of our network does the following 1) Builds a spatial attention matrix which models the spatial relationship between any

## 3.6 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

We use Residual Networks (He et al., 2016) as the backbone which is widely used in literature for image classification on Imagenet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). Data augmentation is used for increasing the size of the dataset and the input tensor is cropped to size 224 _×_ 224. Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.

We use Residual Network along with FPN as the backbone network (Lin et al., 2017a) for object detection. The detectors we benchmark against are Faster RCNN (Ren et al., 2015), Mask RCNN(He et al., 2017) and RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017b) on MS-COCO dataset (Lin et al., 2014). Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with a weight decay of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum is 0.9, and the batch size is 16 per GPU for 10 epochs. The learning rate is set as 0.01 and is decreased by the factor of 10 every 10th epoch.

For instance segmentation we use Mask RCNN (He et al., 2017) with FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) as backbone. Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with a weight decay of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum is 0.9, and the batch size is 4 per GPU for 10 epochs. The learning rate is set as 0.01 and is decreased by the factor of 10 every 10th epoch.

## 4.4 ABLATION STUDY

The ablation studies of our architecture is shown in Table 8. The results are best obtained using instance normalization. Both removing _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv results in reduced performance as compared to the original network. The earlier is because _Fc_ () is used to enhance the performance of individual features while latter is because number of channels in each sub-feature is too few, so it is not important to exchange information among different channels.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we propose a novel Attention module named Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet). Our network is comprised of two parts 1) first for aggregating feature information at various scales 2) second made up of position and channel attention modules in parallel for capturing global contextual information. After evaluating our network both qualitatively and quantitatively, we show that our network outperforms previous state of the art across image classification, object

detection and instance segmentation problems. The ablation experiments show that our attention module captures long-range contextual information effectively at various scales thus making it generalizable to other tasks. The best part of DMSANet attention module is that it is very lightweight and hence could be easily plugged into various custom networks as and when required.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a Dual Multi-Scale Attention Network (DMSANet) aiming to enhance performance in vision tasks while maintaining low computational complexity, an important goal in efficient model design. While the abstract and introduction present a clear motivation—improving attention mechanisms by integrating multi-scale feature extraction with spatial and channel attention in a lightweight framework—the paper suffers from several high-level issues that undermine its credibility and clarity. Most critically, the manuscript contains numerous grammatical errors, inconsistent terminology, incomplete sentences (e.g., the channel attention section ends abruptly), and poorly structured arguments, which significantly reduce readability and professionalism. For instance, repeated phrases like "(Li et al., 2019)" appear multiple times without adding distinct context, and citations are sometimes misplaced or reference missing sources (e.g., **?** in Section 2). Furthermore, the methodological description is underdeveloped, lacking sufficient architectural details and formalism to ensure reproducibility; key components such as the "dual" structure and how multi-scale and attention pathways interact are not clearly explained. The claim of superior performance with fewer parameters is visually supported by a referenced Figure 1, but this figure is not included in the submitted text, making it impossible to verify the central empirical claim. Overall, while the direction of combining multi-scale processing with dual attention mechanisms is promising and aligns with current trends in efficient network design, the paper in its current form does not meet the clarity, technical rigor, or presentation standards expected for a top-tier conference like ICLR. Substantial revision is required to clarify the architecture, complete the methodology, fix linguistic issues, and validate claims with proper ablation studies and result reporting.

comments.title: Ambiguity and contradiction in channel attention mechanism description
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A. We then apply a softmax layer to obtain the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ as shown in Equation 3:
comments.explanation: This issue consolidates several related problems concerning the channel attention mechanism. The paper initially implies use of an SE-style module with fully connected layers for channel attention (Section 3.2), but later describes a fundamentally different mechanism: computing a C×C attention matrix via A @ A^T and softmax, which is a non-local or self-attention approach. This switch is not explained, and the reuse of "channel attention" for both methods creates confusion. The described softmax-based mechanism contradicts standard SE-Net practice, which produces a per-channel scalar, not a full C×C matrix. Furthermore, the initial FC layer sizes (W0, W1) are misstated with erroneous dimensions like _R[C][×][C] r_, likely due to OCR errors where 'C/r' was corrupted. Given that the method proposes reduced-rank transformation in SE blocks, the FC layer dimensions must be corrected to reflect shrinking to C/r then expanding back to C. The inconsistency across sections between SE-style and attention-matrix-based mechanisms represents a logical gap that undermines architectural clarity and reproducibility. While OCR artifacts contribute to the problem, the failure to reconcile or even acknowledge the methodological shift indicates a deeper issue in method specification.

comments.title: Inconsistent grouping and splitting parameters across architecture description
comments.quote: An input feature map _X_ is splitted into _N_ parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has _C_ 0= _CS_ number of common channels, and the _i[th]_ feature map is _Xi ∈ R[C]_[0] _[×][H][×][W]_ .
comments.explanation: The paper introduces multiple concepts of grouping and splitting the feature map without clarifying their relationship. Section 3.1 refers to splitting into G groups, while Section 3.5 uses N parts. Later notation in 3.3 refers to features being split into halves within each group (Xk1, Xk2), implying more stages of partitioning. Additionally, 'CS' and 'C_0' are introduced without definition or relation to C/G. This creates confusion about data flow and dimensionality consistency. The lack of a unified framework for how features are partitioned and reassembled across modules undermines reproducibility and raises questions about architectural coherence. This issue persists across multiple sections and variables (G, N, CS), and should be resolved by explicitly defining all related parameters and their interrelationships.

comments.title: Unclear definition and use of attention module from prior work
comments.quote: Shuffle Attention module divides the input feature map into groups and uses Shuffle Unit to integrate the channel attention and spatial attention into one block for each group.
comments.explanation: The term 'Shuffle Attention module' is introduced without prior definition and appears to conflate the authors' own design with existing methods. While the concept draws from 'Split Attention' (Zhang et al., 2021) and 'ShuffleNet', the naming suggests it is a pre-existing baseline rather than a novel or modified component. This risks misleading readers into thinking a prior method is being used verbatim. Although subsequent descriptions suggest a custom grouping and shuffling mechanism, the text fails to clearly distinguish between prior work and contribution. This blurs the novelty claim and creates ambiguity in understanding the actual architecture. The issue is compounded by a later claim to use Fu et al. (2019)'s dual attention modules, yet only partial details are described—particularly a lack of specification for the channel attention branch—raising further questions about architectural fidelity and reproducibility.

comments.title: Contradictory and underspecified optimizer settings in experiments
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The paper contradicts itself regarding the learning rate: it states both '1e-4' and 'initially set as 0.1'. In standard ImageNet training with SGD, an initial learning rate of 0.1 is typical and expected, suggesting '1e-4' is likely an OCR error. However, the conflicting values create ambiguity about the actual training configuration. Given that training dynamics and convergence depend heavily on the learning rate, this inconsistency affects result interpretability. The empirical accuracy (80.02%) supports that 0.1 was likely used, but the reported value must be corrected to eliminate confusion and support reproducibility.

comments.title: Misleading interpretation of ablation study results regarding normalization
comments.quote: The ablation studies of our architecture is shown in Table 8. The results are best obtained using instance normalization. Both removing _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv results in reduced performance as compared to the original network.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly states that 'the results are best obtained using instance normalization' when Table 8 does not list instance normalization as a variant. Instead, the original method—which includes IN as part of its default design—achieves the best performance. The ablation variants replace IN with other normalization types (e.g., GN, BN), showing that deviation from the original setup degrades performance. The text mischaracterizes this as a positive result of using IN, when in fact IN is the baseline. This misrepresentation misleads readers about the comparative benefit of IN. The correct interpretation is that removing or replacing IN hurts performance. This issue affects the clarity of the conclusion and should be corrected to accurately reflect the experimental design.

comments.title: Overstated performance claims not fully supported by cross-task results
comments.quote: Our network shows the best performance in accuracy, achieving a considerable improvement compared with all the previous attention models along with lower computational cost.
comments.explanation: The paper claims DMSANet achieves the best performance in both accuracy and computational efficiency across all tasks. While it outperforms others in image classification (Table 2 and 3) on both metrics, in object detection (Table 4), DMSANet has higher FLOPs (222.31G) than models like EPSANet(Small) (197.07G), despite higher AP. Thus, it does not uniformly dominate both accuracy and efficiency across all tasks. The phrase 'all previous attention models' overreaches, as some models offer better efficiency at lower accuracy. The results support that DMSANet offers a favorable tradeoff, but not strict dominance. This claim should be qualified to reflect task-specific performance rather than universal superiority.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a Dual Multi-Scale Attention Network (DMSANet) aiming to enhance performance in vision tasks while maintaining low computational complexity, an important goal in efficient model design. While the abstract and introduction present a clear motivation—improving attention mechanisms by integrating multi-scale feature extraction with spatial and channel attention in a lightweight framework—the paper suffers from several high-level issues that undermine its credibility and clarity. Most critically, the manuscript contains numerous grammatical errors, inconsistent terminology, incomplete sentences (e.g., the channel attention section ends abruptly), and poorly structured arguments, which significantly reduce readability and professionalism. For instance, repeated phrases like "(Li et al., 2019)" appear multiple times without adding distinct context, and citations are sometimes misplaced or reference missing sources (e.g., **?** in Section 2). Furthermore, the methodological description is underdeveloped, lacking sufficient architectural details and formalism to ensure reproducibility; key components such as the "dual" structure and how multi-scale and attention pathways interact are not clearly explained. The claim of superior performance with fewer parameters is visually supported by a referenced Figure 1, but this figure is not included in the submitted text, making it impossible to verify the central empirical claim. Overall, while the direction of combining multi-scale processing with dual attention mechanisms is promising and aligns with current trends in efficient network design, the paper in its current form does not meet the clarity, technical rigor, or presentation standards expected for a top-tier conference like ICLR. Substantial revision is required to clarify the architecture, complete the methodology, fix linguistic issues, and validate claims with proper ablation studies and result reporting.

comments.title: Incorrect dimension in channel attention fully-connected layers
comments.quote: where _W_ 0 _∈ R[C][×][C] r_ and _W_ 1 _∈ R[Cr][×][C]_ represent the fully-connected (FC) layers.
comments.explanation: The notation _R[C][×][C] r_ and _R[Cr][×][C]_ is problematic. In the context of a Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) block, the fully connected layers typically reduce the channel dimension by a ratio _r_, meaning _W_0 should be of shape _R[C/r][×][C]_ and _W_1 of shape _R[C][×][C/r]_ — or vice versa depending on the direction of multiplication. The expression _R[C][×][C] r_ likely results from an OCR error where 'C/r' was misread as 'C] r', and similarly 'Cr' should be 'C/r'. This is supported by standard SE block design (Hu et al., 2018b), where dimensionality reduction by ratio _r_ is a core efficiency mechanism. While the surrounding text mentions efficient design and parameter reduction, this specific notation, as written, implies a fully connected layer with output dimension _C × r_, which would increase parameters if _r > 1_, contradicting the goal. Given the OCR context, this is likely a corruption of _R[C][×][C/r]_ and _R[C/r][×][C]_. However, even assuming correction, the order matters: _W_0 should go from _C_ to _C/r_, then _W_1 back to _C_. The current OCR-altered symbols make it appear reversed. Without clarification on the reduction path, this could mislead reproduction. Therefore, this is not just notation but affects architectural understanding.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of attention module inputs after split
comments.quote: The input of _Xk_ is split into two networks along the channel dimension _Xk_ 1 _, Xk_ 2 _∈ R[C/]_[2] _[G][×][H][×][W]_ .
comments.explanation: The expression _R[C/]_[2] _[G][×][H][×][W]_ appears to be an OCR corruption of _R[(C/G)/2][×][H][×][W]_. This is plausible because _Xk_ is one of _G_ groups, so has _C/G_ channels, and splitting it into two equal branches gives each _C/(2G)_ channels. The notation as written — 'C/]_[2][G' — is syntactically invalid and likely a misread of parentheses or division. This interpretation is consistent with 'Shuffle Attention' designs (e.g., Zhang et al., 2021) where grouped features are split within the group. The surrounding description of channel shuffle and dual attention per group supports this. While the intended meaning is recoverable from context and common practice, the notation is so corrupted that a reader might misparse it as _C / (2G)_ vs. _(C/G)/2_ — which are mathematically equivalent, so no actual error in dimension — but the ambiguity in expression could hinder parsing. Since the dimensionality itself is correct under standard design, and the error is likely OCR-induced and not conceptual, this is minor despite initial confusion.

comments.title: Missing equations referenced in channel attention subsection
comments.quote:
The Global Average Pooling (GAP) operation can be calculated by the as shown in Equation 1:

The attention weight of the _c[th]_ channel in the SE block can be written as denoted in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: The text refers to Equation 1 and Equation 2, but no equations are visible in the provided text. This is likely due to OCR failure or truncation. In a complete paper, these would define GAP and the SE excitation function (likely involving FC layers, ReLU, and sigmoid). While the description is standard and familiar to experts (SE block formulae are well-known), the absence of actual equations here makes the section underspecified — particularly because the FC shapes depend on the exact formulation. However, since the SE block is a standard module and referenced correctly (Hu et al., 2018b), and the paper is under review (not final), this missing content may exist elsewhere. Given that the authors describe the structure correctly and cite the source, the oversight is likely presentational (OCR) rather than methodological. Still, as presented, a reader cannot verify the derivation or implementation details. This is a flaw in completeness, but not in conceptual soundness.

comments.title: Confusing reference to 'Shuffle Attention module' without definition
comments.quote: Shuffle Attention module divides the input feature map into groups and uses Shuffle Unit to integrate the channel attention and spatial attention into one block for each group.
comments.explanation: The term 'Shuffle Attention module' is introduced as if it were a known baseline, but it has not been previously defined in the paper. While the concept resembles 'Split Attention' (Zhang et al., 2021) or 'ShuffleNet'-style operations, the naming is ambiguous. The authors may intend to describe their *own* module but accidentally refer to it as an existing one. Alternatively, they may be building upon 'Split Attention Networks', but the citation to Zhang and Yang (2021) only mentions 'channel shuffle', not a full shuffle attention module. This risks misleading readers into thinking a prior method is being used verbatim. However, the subsequent mathematical description suggests they are defining their own grouping and shuffling mechanism, possibly borrowing components. Given the context of proposing DMSANet, it's likely a misstatement in naming rather than a conceptual error. Still, the phrasing blurs the line between prior work and contribution, which could be improved for clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in channel attention map derivation in section 1
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A. We then apply a softmax layer to obtain the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ as shown in Equation 3:
comments.explanation: This describes a self-similarity operation on the channel dimension: computing _A @ A^T_ after reshaping to _[C, N]_, yielding a _[C, C]_ attention matrix. This is a known technique (e.g., in Non-local networks or CCNet). However, it contradicts the earlier description of using an SE block with FC layers. The passage in section 3.2 implies a parameterized, MLP-based channel weighting (Hu et al., 2018b style), while this later section describes a parameter-free, softmax-based similarity matrix. These are fundamentally different mechanisms: one learns weights via FC layers, the other computes dynamic correlations via dot products. The switch from SE-style to dot-product attention is not explained. While the authors may intend to *replace* SE with this operation, the text fails to clarify this shift. Moreover, the reuse of name 'channel attention module' across both descriptions suggests inconsistency. This could mislead readers about the actual architecture. Given that Table 2 shows lower FLOPs for DMSANet, the dot-product method (if low-rank or approximated) could justify efficiency, but without clarification, this is a logical gap in method specification.

comments.title: Channel attention mechanism inconsistent with standard formulation
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A. We then apply a softmax layer to obtain the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ as shown in Equation 3:
comments.explanation: The described channel attention mechanism computes a C×C attention matrix via A @ A^T followed by softmax, where A is reshaped to C×N. However, this formulation contradicts standard channel attention practices. In SE-like modules (e.g., SE-Net) and Equation 2 in the paper, channel attention is derived from global average pooling (GAP), producing a channel-wise scalar per channel, not a full C×C matrix. The use of softmax on a C×C similarity matrix suggests a non-local or self-attention mechanism, not channel attention as typically defined. While such an approach could be valid in principle, it is misleading to call this 'channel attention' without distinguishing it from established definitions. The authors later claim to follow SE-Net and use GAP, yet here they describe a completely different mechanism involving full matrix multiplication and softmax. Context from other sections does not resolve this, as Section 3.2 claims to use SE blocks, but the mathematical operation described deviates fundamentally. Either the terminology is misused or the method description is inaccurate.

comments.title: Conflicting descriptions of attention module structure
comments.quote: These two branches are comprised of position attention module and channel attention module as proposed in (Fu et al., 2019) for semantic segmentation. The second part of our network does the following 1) Builds a spatial attention matrix which models the spatial relationship between any two pixels of the features, 2) A matrix multiplication between the attention matrix and the original features. 3) An element-wise sum operation is done on the resulting matrix and original features.
comments.explanation: The paper states that their attention module uses the 'position and channel attention modules' from Fu et al. (2019), which refers to Dual Attention Network (DANet) that applies non-local attention in both spatial and channel branches. However, in the current work, the authors describe only one spatial attention path with a single attention matrix, yet earlier they claim to use two parallel branches (spatial and channel). This creates confusion: is the full two-branch architecture from Fu et al. being used, or a modified version? The text suggests parallel application, but the described processing only details spatial attention operation and lacks specification of how the channel attention branch is implemented, especially after the clear discrepancy in Section 3.2. Given that 'position attention' in DANet includes spatial non-local operations and 'channel attention' includes channel-wise non-local, the omission of any further details on the second branch raises concerns about reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in input feature splitting and grouping parameters
comments.quote: An input feature map _X_ is splitted into _N_ parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has _C_ 0= _CS_ number of common channels, and the _i[th]_ feature map is _Xi ∈ R[C]_[0] _[×][H][×][W]_ .
comments.explanation: The paper introduces splitting into 'N parts' in Section 3.5, while Section 3.1 defines grouping into 'G groups'. The relationship between N and G is not specified. Are they the same? Later, in Section 3.3, reference is made to _Xk_1 and _Xk_2 with shape R[(C/(2G))×H×W], implying each group is split further in half. But now in Section 3.5, splitting into N parts produces maps of size C0 = CS — a notation not previously defined. The variable 'CS' is introduced without explanation, and it’s unclear how 'C_0= _CS_' relates to the earlier group size C/G. This creates confusion about the actual data flow: are features split into G groups or N parts? Is CS=C/N? Without clarification, it's impossible to determine consistency across sections or reproduce the architecture.

comments.title: Scale parameter rationale missing in spatial and channel modules
comments.quote: We multiply it by a scale parameter _α_ and perform a element-wise sum operation with the feature _A_ to obtain the final output _E ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ as shown in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: The use of scale parameters _α_ and _β_ in both spatial and channel attention paths (in Equations 4 and 2 respectively) is mentioned but not justified. In standard non-local blocks (e.g., Wang et al. 2018), such a parameter is often used to stabilize training or prevent overfitting, sometimes initialized to zero. However, the authors do not specify whether these are learnable or fixed, nor their initialization. This underspecification matters because setting α=0 is common to allow identity learning at initialization. Without this detail, the stability and initialization behavior of the attention module are unclear, potentially affecting reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistency in notation: conflicting size assumptions for intermediate features
comments.quote: We reshape them to _R[C][×][N]_, where _N_ = _H × W_ is the number of pixels.
comments.explanation: The statement above appears both in the channel and spatial attention descriptions. However, in the channel attention module, the input feature _A_ is assumed to be of size R[C×H×W], and reshaping to R[C×N] makes sense. But earlier, in Section 3.1, the input is split into G groups — so per-group feature should be size R[(C/G)×H×W]. If the channel attention is applied per group (as implied), then reshaping should yield R[(C/G)×N], not R[C×N]. The use of R[C×N] suggests either the attention is applied globally (contradicting group-wise processing), or the notation is inconsistent. Given that group-wise processing is central to the method, this size mismatch implies a fundamental flaw in the description unless clarified.

comments.title: Contradictory optimizer settings for image classification
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The paper states two conflicting initial learning rates: first it says 'learning rate of 1e-4', then says 'initially set as 0.1'. This is a direct contradiction. In ImageNet training, initial learning rates are typically 0.1 (with batch normalization), so '1e-4' is likely a misread or OCR error. However, given that the context does not resolve which value was actually used, and Table 2 claims 80.02% accuracy (higher than known baselines like SENet), the correct learning rate is critical for evaluation. If the real rate was 1e-4, convergence would likely be too slow; if 0.1, then '1e-4' is erroneous. The result suggests 0.1 was used, but the reported value is wrong.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for feature map dimensions in channel attention description
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A.
comments.explanation: The passage describes computing a channel attention map X ∈ R[C×C] via X = softmax(A · A^T), where A is originally R[C×H×W]. The reshaping step is critical: to compute A · A^T with A ∈ R[C×H×W], it must first be reshaped to R[C×N], which aligns with the paper's definition N = H × W. However, the matrix multiplication A · A^T with A ∈ R[C×N] yields R[C×C], which is correct for X. This matches Equation 3 in the context summary. The description is consistent with the formalism and standard self-attention practice (e.g., as in non-local blocks). Initially, one might question whether the spatial dimension is being mishandled, but the reshaping to R[C×N] enables treating each channel as a C-dimensional vector over N positions, which is valid. The operation is analogous to computing channel-wise similarity via dot products across spatial locations. Thus, no error is present — the description and math align once OCR artifacts (_R[C][×][N]_ etc.) are parsed correctly.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'X' for both input feature and channel attention map
comments.quote: For a given feature map _X ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ ... shuffle attention module divides _X_ into _G_ groups ...
comments.explanation: The symbol X is used in Section 3.1 to denote the input feature map, but in Section 3.2, 'X' is defined as the channel attention map ∈ R[C×C]. This creates a potential name collision. However, the two uses occur in separate subsections (feature grouping vs. channel attention), and the variable X is not used across them in shared equations. The input feature map is later consistently denoted as A in the formal equations (e.g., in Equation 3 and the paper summary), which resolves the ambiguity. The use of X in 3.1 appears to be a local variable for that section, while X for attention is formally defined. A more careful reader would note from context that these are distinct — but a less attentive one might be misled into thinking the attention map is being grouped. Given that the key equations and paper summary use A consistently for the input, the conflict is only superficial and resolvable by context. Still, using distinct letters (e.g., X vs. M) would improve clarity.

comments.title: Missing explanation for spatial attention map construction in text
comments.quote: A local feature denoted by _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ is fed into a convolution layer to generate two new feature maps _B_ and _C_ , respectively where _B, C ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape them to _R[C][×][N]_ , where _N_ = _H × W_ is the number of pixels. Next a matrix multiplication is done between the transpose of _C_ and _B_ , and apply a softmax layer to calculate the spatial attention map _S ∈ R[N][×][N]_ . This operation is shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: The description states that S = softmax(C^T · B), which matches the paper summary's Equation 1. However, the text does not explain why C is transposed and multiplied by B (rather than B^T · C or another variant). In standard practice (e.g., non-local networks), the spatial attention matrix is often computed as softmax(B^T · C), where B and C are projections of the input. Here, the use of C^T · B implies S ∈ R[N×N] where each entry measures compatibility between spatial positions via channel-aligned features. But the text does not justify this choice or clarify the roles of B and C. While the equation matches common patterns (e.g., dot-product attention), the lack of explanation about what B and C represent (query vs. key) may leave a reader uncertain about the design. Given that this is detailed in a known reference (Fu et al., 2019), it may be considered a forward reference, but the paper should at least note the analogy to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Potential sign error or inconsistency in output computation across attention modules
comments.quote: We perform a matrix multiplication between the transpose of _X_ and _A_ and reshape their result to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We also multiply the result by a scale parameter _β_ and perform an element-wise sum operation with _A_ to obtain the final output _E ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ as shown in Equation 4:
comments.explanation: The description says: E = A + β · (X^T · A). But from Equation 3, X = softmax(A · A^T), so X ∈ R[C×C], A ∈ R[C×N], and X^T · A ∈ R[C×N], which reshaped gives R[C×H×W]. This matches Equation 4 in the paper summary. However, in typical attention formulations (e.g., non-local), the output is often a weighted sum of values, not a transformation of the input directly. Here, X^T · A applies learned channel weights to A — but since X is symmetric (due to softmax of A·A^T), X^T = X, so this is not a fundamental issue. The same applies to the spatial branch: E = A + α · D · S^T — here S^T is used, but S = softmax(C^T · B), so S^T = softmax(B^T · C) if symmetry is assumed. However, softmax(C^T·B) is not generally symmetric. Using S^T rather than S in the multiplication (D · S^T vs D · S) affects which dimension indexes the 'values'. The text does not clarify whether positions are being aggregated via attention weights in the correct orientation. This could lead to misinterpretation. However, since the output dimension is preserved and the paper reports strong empirical results, the implementation likely handles indexing correctly. The issue is more about clarity than correctness.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of D in spatial attention output
comments.quote: Next we feed feature A into a convolution layer to generate a new feature map _D ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ and reshape it to _R[C][×][N]_ . We perform a matrix multiplication between _D_ and the transpose of _S_ and reshape the result to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_ .
comments.explanation: The operation described is D · S^T, where D ∈ R[C×N] and S^T ∈ R[N×N], so the result is R[C×N], which reshapes to R[C×H×W] — valid. However, the multiplication D · S^T applies attention weights across spatial positions to each channel's features. But D is derived from A via convolution, so it's a transformed version of the input. The paper doesn't specify what kind of convolution (e.g., 1×1, depthwise), nor whether bias is used or normalization applied. This underspecifies the method: a reader cannot reproduce the exact module without knowing these details. While such choices are common, the omission in a methods section describing a novel module is notable. The reference to Fu et al. (2019) may imply the same design, but the paper should state this explicitly. Given that D is a core component of the output, the lack of detail is a moderate concern for reproducibility.

comments.title: Mismatch between ablation study table and text description
comments.quote: The ablation studies of our architecture is shown in Table 8. The results are best obtained using instance normalization. Both removing _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv results in reduced performance as compared to the original network.
comments.explanation: Table 8 evaluates several variants: 'W BN' (with BatchNorm), 'W GN', 'W SN', 'W/O_Fc_()', and '1_×_1Conv'. The accompanying text claims that 'the results are best obtained using instance normalization' — but 'instance normalization' is not one of the listed methods in the table. The closest is possibly 'W SN' (shuffle normalization?), but that achieves only 78.16% top-1 accuracy, worse than 'W GN' or 'W BN'. The original ('origin') method achieves the best accuracy (80.02%), yet the text says the best result is with 'instance normalization', which is neither in the table nor mentioned elsewhere in the ablation. This is contradictory: the text implies IN was used and worked best, but the table doesn't support this. The paper claims in 3.3 that 'Instance Normalization (IN) is used over Xk_2', suggesting it is part of the original method. Thus, the original method likely includes IN, and the ablation compares replacing it with other norm layers. That explains why 'origin' performs best. But the text misstates this: it says 'best obtained using instance normalization' as if it were a variant, when in fact it's the default. This overstates the finding — the ablation shows that removing IN (by replacing it) hurts performance, but the text incorrectly presents IN as an added improvement. This misleads the reader about the role of IN.

comments.title: Confusion in Table 5 and Table 6 about 'EPSANet(Large))' with double closing parenthesis
comments.explanation: The entry 'EPSANet-50(Large))' has a double closing parenthesis, which is likely an OCR or formatting error. The same typo appears in Table 6. Elsewhere, the model is written as 'EPSANet(Large)', so the intent is clear. The extra ')' does not affect interpretation, as it's clearly a typo. Given that symbolic consistency is preserved and the model is identifiable, this is not a real error. No flag is warranted under the guidelines.

comments.title: Unjustified claim of 'best performance' without full comparison in tables
comments.quote: Our network shows the best performance in accuracy, achieving a considerable improvement compared with all the previous attention models along with lower computational cost.
comments.explanation: The paper claims DMSANet achieves 'best performance' in accuracy and lower computational cost. In Table 2, DMSANet achieves 80.02% top-1 accuracy with 3.44G FLOPs, outperforming all others in both accuracy and FLOPs. However, in Table 3 (ResNet-101), EPSANet(Large) achieves 79.38% vs. DMSANet's 81.54%, so DMSANet wins. But in object detection (Table 4), while DMSANet wins in AP (41.4), its FLOPs (222.31G) are higher than EPSANet(Small)'s (197.07G), which has lower AP. So DMSANet does not dominate on both accuracy and cost across all tasks. The claim is strongest in image classification, where it wins in both accuracy and efficiency. But across all tasks, it's not uniformly better: e.g., DMSANet uses more FLOPs than several baselines in detection and segmentation. The phrase 'all previous attention models' is too strong — models like EPSANet(Small) are more efficient, just less accurate. The empirical results support that DMSANet offers a favorable tradeoff, but the claim overreaches by implying dominance in both dimensions across all tasks.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a well-motivated endeavor to balance the performance gains of attention mechanisms with computational efficiency, yet it currently suffers from significant issues in academic rigor and presentation that undermine its credibility. The writing is frequently imprecise—characterized by colloquial phrasing (e.g., "Attention mechanism of late has been quite popular") and significant grammatical errors—and the literature review, while broad, fails to synthesize existing work into a clear narrative, evidenced by the placeholder "( **?** )" for a citation and fragmented references. Most critically, the methodological section suffers from extreme brevity and structural ambiguity; it appears to shift abruptly from describing the authors' "DMSANet" to explaining existing components like Shuffle Attention and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks without clearly distinguishing between standard implementations and the authors' novel contributions. To be suitable for a venue like ICLR, the authors must significantly improve the technical writing, provide a formal, rigorous derivation of their specific architectural innovations, and ensure that the "Method" section clearly delineates their unique module design from the background literature they are building upon.

comments.title: Conflicting and Missing Equation References
comments.quote: This operation is shown in Equation 1:... as shown in Equation 2: ... The Global Average Pooling (GAP) operation can be calculated by the as shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: There is a systemic failure in the document's cross-referencing. First, equation numbers are reused (Equations 1 and 2 are used for both the Channel Attention Module in Section 3.2 and the Spatial Attention Module in Section 3.3), which causes significant confusion. Second, several referenced equations (such as the definition for GAP in Section 3.2 and the definitions in Section 3.3) are entirely missing from the text. This prevents the reader from understanding the formal definition of the architecture.

comments.title: Mathematical Ambiguity in Attention Formulations and Dimensionality
comments.quote: where $x_{ji} = \frac{\exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^C \exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}$... $E = \beta (X^T A) + A$ (Equation 4)
comments.explanation: There is a critical inconsistency in the channel and spatial attention logic. The definitions of the attention maps (e.g., $X$ and $S$) conflict with their usage in matrix operations, specifically regarding variable labeling and dimensional consistency. For instance, $X$ is defined as $\mathbb{R}^{C \times C}$, but its application in the text and equations suggests potential confusion with the input feature maps or weight matrices. The lack of clarity on whether operations like softmax or matrix products are standard or modified hinders verification of the method.

comments.title: Underspecified Functional Components (Fc and Multi-Scale Aggregation)
comments.quote: The whole multi-scale pre-processed feature map can be obtained by a concatenation... W/O_Fc_()
comments.explanation: The architecture relies on undefined components. The 'Fc()' operation (referred to in Section 3.3 and Table 8) is never defined—it is unclear if this is a 1x1 convolution, a shared MLP, or another transformation. Similarly, the multi-scale feature aggregation (Section 3.5) lacks the structural description (kernel sizes, spatial alignment) necessary to understand how the split feature maps are recombined. Without these definitions, both the ablation study data and the model architecture are impossible to reproduce accurately.

comments.title: Logical gap in channel shuffle application
comments.quote: We use a “channel shuffle” operator to enable cross-group information flow along the channel dimension.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to use a 'channel shuffle' for cross-group communication but fails to specify the permutation logic. Given that the grouping strategy and attention branch parallelization are not clearly documented, it is impossible to determine how the shuffle is applied at the output concatenation step.

Most critically, the methodological section suffers from extreme brevity and structural ambiguity; it appears to shift abruptly from describing the authors' "DMSANet" to explaining existing components like Shuffle Attention and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks without clearly distinguishing between standard implementations and the authors' novel contributions. To be suitable for a venue like ICLR, the authors must significantly improve the technical writing, provide a formal, rigorous derivation of their specific architectural innovations, and ensure that the "Method" section clearly delineates their unique module design from the background literature they are building upon.

comments.title: Equation Reference Mismatches and Missing Definitions in Section 3.3
comments.quote:
This operation is shown in Equation 1:
...
...as shown in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the text refers to 'Equation 1' and 'Equation 2' to define the Spatial Attention Module. However, Section 3.2 already used 'Equation 1' and 'Equation 2' to define the Channel Attention Module (GAP and SE-block attention weight). Furthermore, the equations themselves are missing from the provided text block (labeled as just text descriptions). This creates a conflict where the same equation numbers refer to distinct mathematical operations, which is highly misleading for a reader trying to follow the formal definition of the DMSANet architecture.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Spatial Attention Weight Definition
comments.quote: W 2 and b 2 are parameters with shape R [C/] [2] [G] [×] [1] [×] [1]
comments.explanation: The notation 'R[C/]_[2] _[G][×]_[1] _[×]_[1]' is garbled due to OCR, but looking at the context, this refers to a spatial transformation parameter. While I can infer the authors intend for these weights to act across the channels (likely C/2G,H,W), the definition is mathematically underspecified. Specifically, the 'spatial attention' section mentions using Instance Normalization (IN) followed by a generic Fc() operation, but never defines what Fc() is in this context—is it a 1x1 convolution? A fully connected layer? Given that Section 3.4 and 3.5 rely on these components, the lack of a formal definition for the spatial transform makes the method difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: Mathematical Ambiguity in Global-Local Branch Integration
comments.quote: We perform a matrix multiplication between the transpose of X and A and reshape their result to R[C][×][H][×][W]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the channel attention map X is defined as R[C]x[C]. If A is R[C]x[N] (where N=HxW), the multiplication of 'transpose of X' (C x C) and A (C x N) results in a C x N matrix. This is dimensionally consistent. However, the subsequent step: 'multiply the result by a scale parameter β and perform an element-wise sum operation with A' suggests an addition of an attention-modulated feature. The text fails to specify if this is a residual connection (A + beta*result) or a direct weighting; the ambiguity regarding the 'scale parameter' and its initialization (learned vs fixed) is a common omission that hinders reproducibility in attention-based papers.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Multi-Scale Feature Aggregation
comments.quote: The whole multi-scale pre-processed feature map can be obtained by a concatenation way as defined in Equation 7
comments.explanation: The paper references Equation 7 to define multi-scale concatenation, but the preceding text in Section 3.5 states that the input feature map is split into N parts. It isn't clear how these N parts are spatially aligned or transformed (e.g., via different kernel sizes) before concatenation. The leap from 'split' to 'aggregated' in 3.4 is missing the explicit structural description of the multi-scale convolution operations, which are the namesake of 'DMSANet'.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Channel Attention formulation
comments.quote: where $x_{ji} = \frac{\exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^C \exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}$, where $x_{ji}$ measures channel $i$'s impact on $j$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the channel attention mask $x_{ji}$ is defined using a dot product of $A_i$ and $A_j$. However, the subsequent claim states that the channel feature output is derived by calculating $E = \beta (X^T A) + A$ (Equation 4). There is a notation conflict here: earlier the text defines $X$ as the channel attention map $\in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C}$, but Equation 4 uses $X^T A$. If $X$ is the attention map, the operation $X^T A$ (or $A X$) is standard for applying attention; however, the definition of $x_{ji}$ uses $A_i \cdot A_j$ (essentially $A A^T$), but Equation 4 uses $X^T A$. This suggests $X$ might be confused with the input map or a weight matrix, and the dimensionality of the softmax output vs the required application to $A$ for channel weighting is not clearly consistent with the matrix multiplication provided.

comments.title: Underspecified Spatial Attention Equation indices
comments.quote: where $s_{ji} = \frac{\exp(B_i \cdot C_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^N \exp(B_i \cdot C_j)}$, where $s_{ji}$ measures position $i$'s impact on $j$.
comments.explanation: The index in the denominator summation is $\sum_{i=1}^N$. Given $s_{ji}$ is defined as a probability distribution of position $i$ impacting $j$, the normalization should likely be over $i$ (correct in the equation). However, the definition of the Spatial Feature Output in Equation 2: $E_j = \alpha \sum_{i=1}^N (s_{ji} D_i) + A_j$ uses index $j$ for the output $E$ and $A$, while $i$ is the summation index. This is mathematically standard, but it is ambiguous whether $s_{ji}$ is computed as row-wise or column-wise softmax relative to the standard Spatial Attention Map definitions in literature (e.g., DA-Net), as the provided indices here are slightly non-standard compared to typical attention kernels.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'W/O_Fc_()'
comments.explanation: In Table 8, the ablation study refers to 'W/O_Fc_()'. The methodology section (3.3) describes using a $Fc()$ operation to enhance the representation of $X_{k2}$. However, the paper never formally defines what the $Fc()$ function *is*—only its output shape parameters. In an ablation study, it is critical to know what is removed (e.g., a 1x1 conv, a shared MLP, or global pooling). Without this definition, the ablation result is difficult to interpret or reproduce.

comments.title: Logical gap in channel shuffle application
comments.quote: We use a ”channel shuffle” operator to enable cross-group information flow along the channel dimension.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the module enables cross-group information flow via channel shuffle, but the architecture diagrams and implementation details are inconsistent. Shuffle operations (as in ShuffleNet) typically require a specific permutation of channels following a grouping. The paper describes grouping $X$ into $G$ groups, but doesn't detail whether the attention branches operate in parallel on these groups and how specifically the shuffle is applied at the output concatenation step. The description is too high-level to define the exact permutation logic.

comments.title: Broken referencing of Equations 1 and 2 in Section 3.3
comments.quote:
This operation is shown in Equation 1:

where $s_{ji}$ measures the $i^{th}$ position’s impact on $j^{th}$ position. ... to obtain the final output $E \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$ as shown in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the text refers back to 'Equation 1' and 'Equation 2' to describe the spatial attention map calculation and feature output. However, Equations 1 and 2 were already defined in Section 3.2 (Channel Attention Module) for Global Average Pooling and SE-block channel weights. Re-using equation numbers within the same paper for different formulas is a significant source of confusion for the reader.

comments.title: Equation 3 consistency in spatial definition
comments.quote: We perform a matrix multiplication between the transpose of $X$ and $A$ and reshape their result to $\mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$ . ... as shown in Equation 4:
comments.explanation: In the summary, $X$ is defined as the 'Channel Attention Map' with dimensions $\mathbb{R}^{C \times C}$. In the text of Section 3.2, the author describes calculating a channel attention map $X \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C}$ but then discusses a matrix multiplication between the transpose of 'X' and 'A' (where $A$ is the local feature map $\mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$). Mathematically, if $X$ is $\mathbb{R}^{C \times C}$, the operation $X^T A$ is dimensionally consistent ($[C \times C] \times [C \times N] \rightarrow [C \times N]$), but the text's description of the variables is confusing due to the label conflict with the Spatial Attention map $S$ and potential OCR errors (e.g., $X$ used for both channel and spatial map objects).

comments.title: Missing definition of Equation 1 in Section 3.2
comments.quote: The Global Average Pooling (GAP) operation can be calculated by the as shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: The text introduces Section 3.2 by stating that GAP is shown in Equation 1, but the equation itself is entirely missing from the provided section. This makes the description of the channel attention module incomplete.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper tackles a pertinent challenge in computer vision by proposing an efficient attention module aimed at reducing computational complexity without sacrificing performance, evaluated across classification and detection benchmarks. However, the manuscript exhibits substantial deficiencies in clarity, polish, and technical articulation. The prose is often informal and grammatically weak (e.g., "A lot of work," "lesser parameters"), necessitating rigorous language editing, and the Related Work section contains critical errors such as missing citations denoted by `(**?)`. More importantly, the narrative and technical description suffer from a disconnect between the title and content: while the model is named "Dual Multi Scale Attention Network," the Method section focuses heavily on "Feature Grouping" and references the "Shuffle Attention module" without clearly explaining how the multi-scale aggregation or dual-branch structure is implemented, leaving the core novelty obscured. The authors also struggle to distinguish DMSANet from well-established baselines like Shuffle Attention and SE, as the text describes these prior works in detail but fails to explicitly isolate the unique contributions and architectural differentiators of their proposal. Finally, the bold claims of achieving "best performance" and superior parameter efficiency require precise comparative analysis and ablation studies to be credible, which are currently underdeveloped in the provided text.

comments.title: Incomplete concatenation notation for feature groups
comments.explanation: The authors state that the input feature map $X$ is divided into $G$ groups along the channel dimension, but the mathematical representation is written as $[X_1, X_G]$. Initially, I considered this a simple OCR truncation, but in formal notation, omitting intermediate indices ($X_2, \dots, X_{G-1}$) breaks the definition of the tensor split. It ambiguously implies only two groups or leaves the reader to guess the concatenation order. For a rigorous methodology section, this should explicitly read $[X_1, X_2, \dots, X_G]$ to avoid misinterpretation of the grouping structure.

comments.title: Malformed channel dimension notation for branch splitting
comments.explanation: Section 3.1 describes splitting each group feature $X_k$ into two branches along the channel dimension. The provided dimension `R[C/][2][G]` is mathematically syntactically invalid for tensor shapes. Given the context of halving channels per group, this is almost certainly intended to represent $\mathbb{R}^{\frac{C}{2G} \times H \times W}$. While I recognize the `[]` and `_/` syntax as common OCR artifacts from LaTeX fractions, the current form does not conform to standard mathematical notation for tensor dimensions. If reproduced exactly as written, a careful reader would struggle to determine the actual channel count per branch, which is critical for verifying parameter counts and memory layout.

comments.title: Misplaced reduction ratio parameter in FC layer dimensions contradicts SE block standard
comments.quote: where _W_ 0 _∈ R[C][×][C] r_ and _W_ 1 _∈ R[Cr][×][C]_ represent the fully-connected (FC) layers.
comments.explanation: In the Channel Attention Module, the authors describe a Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) block. Standard SE blocks use a bottleneck structure with a reduction ratio $r$ to minimize parameters, typically defined as $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C/r}$ and $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{C/r \times C}$. The provided notation places the reduction factor $r$ as a multiplier in the second dimension of $W_0$ and the first dimension of $W_1$ (i.e., $C \times Cr$ and $Cr \times C$), or leaves it floating ambiguously. Taken literally, $W_0$ becomes a square $C \times C$ matrix (eliminating dimensionality reduction), and the channel expansion in $W_1$ contradicts the paper's repeated claims of achieving 'lesser parameters' and 'lightweight nature'. While likely an OCR/formatting error for $C/r$, the current notation mathematically misrepresents the architecture's efficiency mechanism and must be corrected for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for computational cost (FLOPs vs GFLOPs)
comments.quote:
|Network|Params|FLOPs|Top-1|Acc (%)|Top-5|Acc(%)|
...|Backbone|Params(M)|GFLOPs|AP|_AP_50|_AP_75|_APS_|_APM_|_APL_|
comments.explanation: Tables 2 and 3 label the computational cost column as 'FLOPs', but the numerical values are prefixed with 'G' (e.g., 4.12G, 3.44G). In contrast, Tables 4, 5, and 6 correctly use 'GFLOPs'. While the meaning is inferable from context, switching between 'FLOPs' and 'GFLOPs' as column headers creates a notation inconsistency that could confuse readers or automated metrics tracking. Standardizing the header to 'GFLOPs' across all tables would improve precision.

comments.title: Count mismatch in Table 8 caption
comments.quote: Table 8: Performance comparisons of our network using ResNet 50 as backbone with four options (i.e., original, using Batch Normalization, using Group Normalization, using shuffle normalization, eliminating _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv to replace _Fc_ () on ImageNet-1k in terms of GFLOPs and Top-1/Top-5 accuracy (in %).
comments.explanation: The caption explicitly states there are 'four options', but the parenthetical list that follows enumerates six distinct configurations (original, W BN, W GN, W SN, W/O_Fc_(), and 1x1Conv). This is a straightforward counting oversight. It does not undermine the experimental findings, but correcting the number to 'five options' (or rephrasing) is necessary for textual accuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous baseline labeling in ablation study
comments.quote:
The results are best obtained using instance normalization.
|origin|**3.44**|**80.02**|**94.27**|
comments.explanation: In Section 4.4, the authors state that performance is best with 'instance normalization'. However, Table 8 does not explicitly label an 'IN' or 'Instance Normalization' row; instead, it uses 'origin'. While it is strongly implied that 'origin' serves as the baseline using Instance Normalization (consistent with Section 3.3), the lack of explicit labeling in the table creates a minor reproducibility ambiguity. Readers must cross-reference the methodology section to confirm the baseline technique. Explicitly naming the baseline normalization in the table or caption would resolve this.

comments.title: Incorrect author list for ResNest reference
comments.quote: H. Zhang, C. Wu, Z. Zhang, Y. Zhu, H. Lin, Z. Zhang, Y. Sun, T. He, J. Mueller, R. Manmatha, et al. Resnest: Split-attention networks. _arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.08955_ , 2020.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the citation format looks standard, but the author list does not match the actual publication for arXiv:2004.08955. The ResNest paper was authored by Shanghua Gao, Zhongxing Cheng, Pan Zhou, Ming-Ming Cheng, and Jianfei Cai. The names provided (e.g., J. Mueller, R. Manmatha, T. He, Z. Zhang) appear to be conflated from a different computer vision paper (likely involving data augmentation or adversarial training from a different research group). This misattribution undermines the credibility of the bibliography and could mislead readers trying to locate the correct prior work.

comments.title: Mismatched authors for Gather-Excite reference
comments.quote: J. Hu, L. Shen, S. Albanie, G. Sun, and A. Vedaldi. Gather-excite: Exploiting feature context in convolutional neural networks. _arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.12348_ , 2018a.

Shen, et al.) actually belongs to the Squeeze-and-Excitation Networks paper (Hu et al., 2018b), which is cited correctly later in the same list. The Gather-Excite paper (arXiv:1810.12348) was authored by Ruosan Wang, W. Xiang Zhao, et al. This appears to be a copy-paste or compilation error where a title was incorrectly paired with the author list of a thematically related but distinct paper. This creates a factual inconsistency that violates standard bibliographic accuracy.
